page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.re6.4xlarge is a memory-optimized instance powered by the Intel Xeon 8269CL processor running at 2.5 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. It features 16 dedicated physical cores with a 1:1 thread-to-core ratio and 256.0 GB of RAM, yielding 16.0 GB of memory per core. The processor includes a 35 MB L3 cache and supports AVX-512 vector extensions, including AVX512_VNNI for vector neural network instructions. This instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, and it operates on a KVM hypervisor with a baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates a clear performance profile. Single-core performance is relatively weak, as shown by stress-ng single-core CPU and gzip compression tests falling into the bottom 25% tier. However, multi-core execution scales to average levels, with stress-ng multi-core and bzip3 multi-threaded decompression scoring in the middle 50% range. Memory bandwidth is stable, reaching 783.69 GB/sec for cached small-block reads and 71.81 GB/sec for uncached large-block reads. Database operations and Redis SET performance (12.92 million operations per second) also align with average tier expectations.

From a resource density perspective, the instance offers high memory capacity relative to its 16 vCPUs, making it qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-bound workloads that do not require top-tier single-core CPU speeds. The primary tradeoff is the lower single-threaded processing capability. This profile is appropriate for in-memory databases, caching layers like Redis, medium-scale database operations, and memory-intensive enterprise applications.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.re6.4xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 16 dedicated Intel Xeon 8269CL physical cores running at 2.5 GHz and 256.0 GB of RAM. Operating on a KVM hypervisor, this x86_64 server provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 16 GB per core but lacks local storage and GPU accelerators. Baseline network bandwidth is rated at 5 Gbps. Benchmark results show weak single-core CPU performance, but multi-core operations and memory bandwidth fall into the average tier, with Redis SET operations reaching 12.92 million operations per second. This resource configuration is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-heavy applications where massive RAM capacity is prioritized over raw single-threaded CPU speed. It is suited for in-memory databases, caching layers, and memory-intensive enterprise workloads.
